Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MS):
Read and store the RCPSP instance data: activities, durations, precedence relations, and renewable resource capacities.
Construct the precedence graph and compute the transitive closure to identify all predecessor and successor relationships.
Perform a forward pass to calculate the earliest start time (EST) and earliest finish time (EFT) for each activity using the precedence constraints only.
Perform a backward pass to calculate the latest start time (LST) and latest finish time (LFT) for each activity, assuming an infinite horizon or a given project deadline.
Compute the slack for each activity as Slack = LST - EST (or equivalently LFT - EFT).
Initialize the schedule: set the current time t = 0, mark all activities as unscheduled, and set the available amount of each renewable resource to its capacity.
Create a list of eligible activities: activities whose all predecessors have already been scheduled (or have no predecessors) and are not yet scheduled.
Assign a priority to each eligible activity equal to its slack value (minimum slack gets highest priority).
Sort the eligible activities in ascending order of slack (i.e., smallest slack first).
Iterate over the sorted eligible activities and try to schedule each at the earliest feasible start time ≥ t:
   • Check resource feasibility: for the candidate start time, verify that the required amount of each renewable resource is available for the whole duration of the activity.
   • If resources are insufficient, shift the candidate start time forward to the next time point where resources become available and repeat the feasibility check.
   • Once a feasible start time is found, assign the activity its start time, update the resource availability profile for the activity’s duration, and mark the activity as scheduled.
After scheduling all currently eligible activities, advance the current time t to the earliest finish time among the activities that have started but not yet finished.
Update the list of eligible activities based on newly completed predecessors and repeat steps 7–14 until all activities are scheduled.
Perform a final feasibility check to ensure that no resource capacity is violated at any time point.
Output the schedule: for each activity, provide its start time, finish time, and assigned resources.
Maximum Resource Demand Priority Rule (M:
Load the RCPSP instance: activities, durations, precedence relations, resource requirements per activity, and resource capacities.
Calculate the total resource demand for each activity by summing its requirements across all resource types.
Create a priority list by sorting activities in descending order of their total resource demand (Maximum Resource Demand priority).
Initialize the schedule: mark all activities as unscheduled, set the current time to 0, and set available resources equal to their capacities.
While there are unscheduled activities, repeat the following steps:
 Identify the set of eligible activities whose all predecessor activities have already finished.
 From the eligible set, select the activity with the highest MRD priority that can be started with the currently available resources.
 If such an activity exists, assign it a start time equal to the current time, reserve its required resources for its entire duration, and mark it as scheduled.
 If no eligible activity can start, advance the current time to the earliest finishing time among the activities already in execution and release the resources of the activities that finish at that time.
Update the resource availability after each start or finish event.
After all activities are scheduled, perform a forward pass to finalize start and finish times for every activity.
Optionally apply post‑processing improvements such as schedule compression or idle‑time reduction.
Output the final schedule, including start times, finish times, and the resource usage profile over time.
Validate the schedule to ensure all precedence constraints are satisfied and resource capacities are never exceeded.
Compute and report performance metrics such as makespan, total resource idle time, and algorithm runtime.
Shortest Processing Time Rule (SPT):
Parse the project data: list of activities, their processing times, precedence relations, and renewable/non‑renewable resource requirements and availabilities.
Construct the precedence graph and compute the set of initially eligible activities (those with no unsatisfied predecessors).
Initialize the current time (t) to 0 and create an empty schedule for all activities.
While there are unscheduled activities, repeat the following steps:
 Identify the set of eligible activities at time t (all predecessors finished and not yet scheduled).
 From the eligible set, filter out activities whose resource requirements cannot be met with the currently available resources at time t.
 If the filtered set is empty, advance time t to the earliest finish time of any scheduled activity to release resources, then continue to the next iteration.
 Sort the remaining eligible activities by their processing time (duration) in ascending order (Shortest Processing Time first).
 Select the first activity in the sorted list (the one with the shortest processing time).
 Schedule the selected activity to start at time t, reserve its required resources for its processing time, and record its finish time (t + processing time).
 Update the resource availability profile by reducing the available amount of each resource during the activity’s execution interval.
 Mark the activity as scheduled and remove it from the eligible set.
 Update the eligible set by adding any successor activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled and finished.
 If multiple activities have the same shortest processing time, break ties using a secondary rule (e.g., highest resource demand or earliest due date).
After all activities are scheduled, output the start times, finish times, and resource usage profile as the final schedule.
Longest Processing Time Rule (LPT):
Read and parse the RCPSP instance data: activity list, processing times, precedence relations, renewable and non‑renewable resource capacities.
Compute the processing time (duration) for each activity and store it in a data structure (e.g., a dictionary keyed by activity ID).
Create a list of all activities (excluding dummy start/end activities) and sort it in descending order of processing time (Longest Processing Time first).
Initialize the schedule: set the start time of every activity to undefined, and create a resource availability profile for each renewable resource (initially equal to its total capacity at each time unit).
Maintain a set of activities whose predecessors have all been scheduled (eligible activities). Initially, this set contains only activities whose only predecessor is the dummy start activity.
Iterate over the sorted activity list:
    For the current activity, if it is not yet eligible, skip it and revisit later after its predecessors are scheduled.
    Determine the earliest feasible start time by scanning the time horizon from time 0 onward:
        • Check that all predecessor activities finish before the candidate start time (respect precedence constraints).
        • Verify that for every time unit within the candidate start time to start time + duration, the required amount of each renewable resource is available in the resource profile.
    Assign the activity’s start time to the earliest feasible time found and compute its finish time.
    Update the resource availability profile by subtracting the activity’s resource demands for each time unit of its execution.
    Add all successor activities whose all predecessors are now scheduled to the eligible set.
Continue the iteration until all activities have been assigned start times.
Perform a final consistency check: ensure that all precedence relations are satisfied and that resource capacities are never exceeded at any time point.
Output the resulting schedule (e.g., a table of activity IDs, start times, finish times) and compute performance metrics such as makespan.
Optionally, apply a local improvement or repair step to reduce makespan if the LPT heuristic leaves idle resource periods.
Minimum Latest Finish Time Rule (LFT):
Parse the RCPSP instance: read activities, durations, resource demands, precedence relations, and resource capacities.
Build the precedence graph and verify that it is acyclic.
Perform a forward pass to compute the earliest start (ES) and earliest finish (EF) times for all activities ignoring resource limits.
Perform a backward pass to compute the latest finish (LF) and latest start (LS) times for all activities using the project makespan from the forward pass.
Calculate the total slack for each activity as Slack = LF - EF.
Initialize the set of unscheduled activities U with all activities and create an empty schedule S.
While U is not empty, repeat:
   Identify the eligible set E ⊆ U consisting of activities whose all predecessors are already scheduled in S.
   From E, select the activity a with the minimum latest finish time (minimum LF value).
   Determine the earliest feasible start time t for activity a by scanning the time horizon from its ES forward until the required resources are available for its entire duration.
   Schedule activity a at start time t, record its start and finish times in S, and update the cumulative resource profile to reflect the resources consumed during [t, t + duration_a).
   Remove activity a from U.
After all activities are scheduled, compute the project makespan as the maximum finish time among all activities in S.
Optionally, evaluate schedule quality (e.g., total resource idle time) and apply improvement heuristics if desired.
Minimum Earliest Start Time Rule (EST):
1. Gather all project data: activities, durations, resource requirements, precedence relations, and resource availabilities.
2. Compute the precedence graph and verify it is acyclic.
3. Perform a forward pass to calculate the earliest start time (EST) for each activity ignoring resource constraints.
4. Initialize the schedule with all activities unscheduled and set the current time t = 0.
5. Identify the set of eligible activities: unscheduled activities whose predecessors have all been scheduled and finished.
6. For each eligible activity, retrieve its EST value computed in step 3.
7. Select the activity (or activities) with the minimum EST among the eligible set (the Minimum EST rule).
8. Check resource feasibility: determine if sufficient renewable resources are available at time t to start the selected activity(ies).
9. If resources are sufficient, schedule the selected activity(ies) to start at max(t, its EST) and update their finish times (start + duration).
10. If resources are insufficient, postpone the start of the selected activity(ies) to the earliest future time when enough resources become available, then schedule them.
11. Update the resource profile by reserving the required resources for the scheduled activity(ies) over their execution intervals.
12. Advance the current time t to the earliest finish time of any scheduled but not yet completed activity.
13. Mark activities that have completed at time t as finished, and return to step 5 to update the eligible set.
14. Repeat steps 5–13 until all activities are scheduled.
15. Perform a final feasibility check to ensure all precedence and resource constraints are satisfied, and compute the makespan of the generated schedule.
Minimum Total Float Rule (TF):
1. Read and store the RCPSP instance data: activities, durations, precedence relations, renewable resource capacities, and resource requirements per activity.
2. Perform a forward pass to compute the earliest start (ES) and earliest finish (EF) times for all activities using the precedence constraints.
3. Perform a backward pass to compute the latest start (LS) and latest finish (LF) times for all activities, starting from the project makespan obtained in the forward pass.
4. Calculate the total float (TF) for each activity as TF = LS – ES (or TF = LF – EF).
5. Initialize the schedule: set the current time to 0, mark all activities as unscheduled, and create an empty resource profile for each renewable resource.
6. Identify the set of eligible activities: unscheduled activities whose all predecessors are already scheduled (or have no predecessors).
7. From the eligible set, select the activity (or activities) with the minimum total float value. If there is a tie, break it using a secondary rule (e.g., shortest processing time or highest resource demand).
8. Determine the earliest feasible start time for the selected activity by scanning forward from the current time and checking the resource profile to ensure that adding the activity’s resource requirements does not exceed any resource capacity during its duration.
9. Schedule the selected activity at the earliest feasible start time, update its actual start and finish times, and mark it as scheduled.
10. Update the resource profile by adding the activity’s resource consumption to the corresponding time slots for the duration of the activity.
11. Update the current time to the earliest start time of the next unscheduled activity (or keep it unchanged if multiple activities can start at the same time).
12. Re‑evaluate the eligible activity set (step 6) with the newly scheduled activity taken into account.
13. Repeat steps 6–12 until all activities are scheduled.
14. Compute the final project makespan as the maximum actual finish time among all activities and output the complete schedule (start/finish times for each activity).
Minimum Number of Successors Rule (MNS):
Identify all activities, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence relationships in the RCPSP instance.
Compute the number of immediate successors for each activity based on the precedence graph.
Sort the activities in ascending order of their number of immediate successors; activities with fewer successors get higher priority.
If multiple activities have the same number of successors, break ties using secondary criteria such as shortest processing time or highest resource demand.
Initialize the schedule with time = 0 and an empty list of scheduled activities.
Maintain a list of eligible activities whose predecessors have all been scheduled and are not yet scheduled themselves.
At each scheduling iteration, select the highest‑priority eligible activity according to the sorted order from step 3 (or tie‑breaker from step 4).
Determine the earliest feasible start time for the selected activity by checking resource availability over its duration, respecting renewable resource capacities.
If the activity cannot start at the current time, advance the time to the next earliest point where sufficient resources become available and repeat the feasibility check.
Schedule the activity at its earliest feasible start time, update the resource profile to reflect its consumption, and add its successors to the eligible list when all their predecessors are now scheduled.
Repeat steps 6–10 until all activities have been scheduled, ensuring that precedence constraints are never violated.
Output the final schedule, including start and finish times for each activity, and compute performance metrics such as makespan and resource utilization.
Maximum Number of Successors Rule (MXS):
Read and parse the RCPSP instance data, including the set of activities, their durations, resource requirements, renewable resource capacities, and precedence relations (predecessor/successor lists).
Construct the adjacency list for the project network to identify all direct successors of each activity.
For each activity, compute its total number of successors (direct and indirect) by performing a depth‑first search (DFS) or breadth‑first search (BFS) from that activity and counting all reachable nodes.
Create a priority list by sorting activities in descending order of their total successor count; ties can be broken by secondary criteria such as longer duration or higher resource demand.
Initialize the schedule: set the current time to 0, mark all activities as unscheduled, and create an empty list of scheduled activities.
Maintain a list of eligible activities – those whose all predecessors have already been scheduled (i.e., precedence constraints satisfied). Initially, this list contains the source activities (with no predecessors).
Iterate while there are unscheduled activities:
  a. From the eligible list, select the activity with the highest priority according to the MXS ordering (i.e., the one with the largest number of successors).
  b. Determine the earliest feasible start time for the selected activity by scanning forward from the current time and checking renewable resource availability for each time unit over the activity's duration.
  c. If the activity can be feasibly placed, assign its start time, update the resource profile by reserving the required amounts for the occupied time slots, and mark the activity as scheduled.
  d. Remove the scheduled activity from the eligible list and add any of its immediate successors whose all predecessors are now scheduled to the eligible list.
  e. If no eligible activity can be scheduled at the current time due to resource conflicts, advance the current time to the next earliest time point where at least one eligible activity becomes feasible.
After all activities are scheduled, compute the project makespan as the maximum of (start time + duration) over all activities.
Optionally, perform a post‑processing improvement step such as local search or schedule compression to try to reduce the makespan while preserving feasibility.
Output the final schedule, including start times, finish times, and resource usage profile, together with the makespan and any performance statistics (e.g., number of priority list evaluations).
Minimum Resource Slack Rule (MRS):
Parse the RCPSP instance, reading activities, durations, precedence relations, and renewable resource capacities.
Construct the activity network (directed acyclic graph) based on precedence constraints.
Initialize earliest start times (EST) for all activities using a forward pass through the network.
Initialize latest finish times (LFT) for all activities using a backward pass assuming infinite resources.
Compute the total resource demand for each activity and the total available renewable resource per time period.
Calculate the resource slack for each activity: ResourceSlack(i) = min_{t in [EST_i, LFT_i - d_i]} (AvailableResources_t - Demand_i).
Rank activities by increasing resource slack; activities with the smallest slack have the highest priority (Minimum Resource Slack Rule).
Select the first unscheduled activity in the priority list that satisfies all precedence constraints and does not cause resource overload when scheduled at its earliest feasible start time.
If the activity cannot be placed at its earliest feasible start, shift its start time forward to the earliest time where enough resources are available, respecting precedence constraints.
Insert the activity into the partial schedule and update the resource profile (cumulative resource usage over time).
Re‑compute resource slacks for the remaining unscheduled activities because the resource profile has changed.
Repeat steps 7‑11 until all activities are scheduled, producing a feasible schedule.
Perform a local improvement phase (optional): iterate over scheduled activities, try to shift them earlier or later within their slack windows to reduce makespan while maintaining feasibility.
If a better makespan is found, update the best schedule; otherwise terminate.
Output the final schedule: start time, finish time for each activity, and the makespan.
